coredata.dc:title: MoS2 nanostructured materials for electrode modification in the development of a laccase based amperometric biosensor for non-invasive dopamine detection

Hickey, David P.

García-García, Alejandra
coredata.dc:description: A novel laccase-based amperometric biosensor was developed through the modification of carbon paper electrodes with layered two-dimensional molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) presented in two different morphologies, flowers (MoS2-F) and ribbons (MoS2-R). Two laccase isoforms from Pycnoporus sanguineus CS43 fungi (LacI and LacII) were evaluated for the first time as a biological recognition element, and their performance was compared with commercial laccase from Trametes versicolor (TvL). The best results were observed with the MoS2-R morphology at a concentration of 2 mg/mL Biosensors modified with MoS2-R and with the three enzymes, showed two linear ranges of detection, from 0.1 to 0.5 µM and from 1 to 5 µM. Biosensors modified with LacII exhibited a high sensitivity of 340.3 nA/µM; and low limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM, which are the best values reported in the literature for dopamine detection by laccase based biosensor. Optimized electrodes modified with enzymes LacII and TvL were tested in a synthetic urine sample in order to evaluate their practical effectiveness, resulting in LOD of 0.67 and 2.67 µM respectively, which are below the average concentrations of dopamine normally found in real urine samples (around 2 µM).

Results of c) the S2p spectra fitting into S chemical groups and d) results of the Mo3d spectra fitting into S and Mo chemical groups from MoS2-F.

head.ce:title: MoS2 nanostructured materials for electrode modification in the development of a laccase based amperometric biosensor for non-invasive dopamine detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel laccase-based amperometric biosensor was developed through the modification of carbon paper electrodes with layered two-dimensional molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) presented in two different morphologies, flowers (MoS2-F) and ribbons (MoS2-R). Two laccase isoforms from Pycnoporus sanguineus CS43 fungi (LacI and LacII) were evaluated for the first time as a biological recognition element, and their performance was compared with commercial laccase from Trametes versicolor (TvL). The best results were observed with the MoS2-R morphology at a concentration of 2 mg/mL Biosensors modified with MoS2-R and with the three enzymes, showed two linear ranges of detection, from 0.1 to 0.5 µM and from 1 to 5 µM. Biosensors modified with LacII exhibited a high sensitivity of 340.3 nA/µM; and low limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM, which are the best values reported in the literature for dopamine detection by laccase based biosensor. Optimized electrodes modified with enzymes LacII and TvL were tested in a synthetic urine sample in order to evaluate their practical effectiveness, resulting in LOD of 0.67 and 2.67 µM respectively, which are below the average concentrations of dopamine normally found in real urine samples (around 2 µM).

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical biosensors are self-contained integrated devices, which are capable of providing specific quantitative or semi-quantitative analytical information using a biological recognition element [1]. Also, such devices are of easy construction and operation, economic, with high sensitivity and selectivity [2]. These devices measure chemical substances by means of a sensing element of biological nature (enzymes, nucleic acids, antibodies, whole cells, etc.) [3]. Their construction is based on the combination of a biological component, which recognizes or detects a particular compound in the sample; a transducer or coupled physical element, which is responsible for transforming the biochemical reaction into a measurable signal; and an instrument in which the signals will be recorded legibly [4]. The selection of components plays a very important role in the performance of the biosensor [5].

In recent years, considerable attention has been given to the application of nano and micro-structured materials such as carbon-based, metal, metal alloy and metal oxide nanoparticles for being used as electrochemical transducer [6]. This interest is mainly because these materials present unique properties such as high active surface areas, catalytic activity, chemical stability and good conductivity [7–10]. Recently, two-dimensional (2D) layered transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have been subject to intensive research due to their similarities with graphene as they also present interesting structural, physicochemical, electronic, optical, thermal and mechanical properties [10].

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), a member of the TMDs family, is the most studied graphene-like 2D nanomaterial for a wide range of applications including the development of biosensors, sensors, photocatalysts, transistors and supercapacitors [9,11]. The MoS2 crystals are composed of layers created by a Mo atom covalently bonded to two S atoms (S-Mo-S). These layers are stacked together through weak Van der Waals interactions making it easy to obtain single-layer or few-layers MoS2 materials [9,11–13]. Due to its interesting properties, two-dimensional MoS2 has been used as a transducer modifier element in a series of sensors for the detection of a wide range of analytes such as NO [14], dopamine [15,16] and bisphenol A [7]. Similarly, MoS2 transducers have been coupled to different macromolecules, mainly aptamers, for the construction of field-effect transistors and fluorescence biosensors for the detection of different molecules of interest [13,17–19].

Despite of all the excellent characteristics that 2D nanomaterials present, its application in the construction of enzymatic electrochemical biosensors are in its initial stages [20] as there are only a limited number of reports where the immobilization of enzymes onto MoS2 modified electrodes has been explored [11,21–24], leaving a great field of research to be investigated as it is well-known that when enzymes are used for the construction of biosensors it is possible to obtain devices with enhanced sensitivity, selectivity and good reproducibility [25].

On the other hand, laccases are well-known multicopper polyphenol oxidases that can catalyze the oxidation of phenolic compounds into their quinone form accompanied by the reduction of molecular oxygen [26–28]. Particularly, Ramirez et al. [29] conducted a study where they report the production and characterization of two laccase isoforms (LacI and LacII) from a native strain of the white-rot fungi known as Pycnoporus sanguineus CS43 and found that both isoforms exhibit high thermal stability as they retained their activity upon incubation at 60 °C and have an increased resistance to inhibitors such as chloride, methanol and acetonitrile. In a later study, it was reported that these two isoforms have onset potentials of over +650 mV vs Ag/AgCl at pH 4 [30], which are amongst the highest reported for a laccase bioelectrode making these two isoforms excellent candidates to be used in the fabrication of bioelectrodes.

Dopamine (DA) belongs to the family of catecholamines that can be detected by these laccase enzymes [31–33]. It has been cataloged as a very important biomarker of diseases in the endocrine glands such as multiple endocrine neoplasm II (MEN II) and the presence of tumors like pheochromocytoma or medullary carcinoma of the thyroid [34–37]. The analysis of this catecholamine neurotransmitter has been carried out via urine analysis due to some advantages such as its non-invasiveness and low cost [38]. DA is normally found in urine at concentrations of 0.3–2.6 μM/day, and variations of its normal level can cause paroxysmal or persistent hypertension which could be associated with the diseases mentioned above [36]. DA quantification is usually performed by established analytical techniques such as liquid chromatography and capillary electrophoresis. Despite its low detection limits, these techniques have some important limitations such as large equipment and long analysis times [39]. For these reasons, it is of such importance to develop biosensors for faster and more convenient detection of this biomarker than conventional techniques.

To the best of our knowledge, there is only one report available in literature where a laccase enzyme from Trametes versicolor (TvL) was immobilized onto electrodes modified with MoS2, which responded efficiently to determine some polyphenolic compounds, such as caffeic acid, in red wine [24]. However, they did not explore the effect that the morphology of MoS2 has on the performance of the electrodes. It has been established that the morphology and composition of nanomaterials have a great influence on electrode properties, arising great opportunities for tailoring and enhancing their performance as electrode modifiers [40].

Herein, we report for the first time the development of an electrochemical biosensor for DA detection by employing two laccase isoforms (LacI and LacII) obtained from the white-rot fungi Pycnoporus sanguineus CS43 as biological recognition element, as well as two different nanostructured morphologies of MoS2 (MoS2-F and MoS2-R) as part of the transducer element. Furthermore, since LacI and LacII isoforms have never been used in the fabrication of a biosensor, the commercially available enzyme laccase from TvL, which is one of the most reported in the literature, was also tested as a point of comparison for the performance of LacI and LacII.

Laccase (benzenediol: oxygen oxidoreductase EC 1.10.3.2, activity provided on the bottle ≥10 U/mg) from Trametes versicolor (TvL), potassium hydroxide, dopamine hydrochloride, dibasic sodium phosphate, ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate (AHMo), thiourea (TU), molybdenum (VI) oxide (MoO3) and sulfur powder were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid monohydrate was purchased from Fisher Scientific. Toray Carbon Paper (TGP-H-060) was purchased from Fuel Cell Earth. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification and all solutions were prepared using deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm).

The two isoforms of laccase from P. sanguineus CS43 (LacI and LacII) were obtained from a tomato medium as described in our previous work [29]. Briefly, mycelia were recovered from the tomato medium supernatant after 10 days of culture by filtration (0.2 μm pore size). Then, it was concentrated by ultrafiltration with a tangential-flow filter (Membrane cut-off of 10 kDa, Sartorius Sartojet). The ultra-filtered sample was purified with a DEAE-cellulose ion exchange column eluted with 20 to 300 mM phosphate buffer pH 6.0 at a flow rate of 2 mL/min. Finally, laccase fractions were collected and concentrated.

The synthesis of MoS2-R was done following the procedure reported by Najmaei et al. [41] with certain modifications. The modified synthesis is registered under patent MX-a-2017-016742 [42]. Firstly, MoO3 and sulfur powder were put into a tube furnace at 600 °C under N2 flow. Afterwards, a post-treatment via recrystallization was carried out at 800 °C for 1 h. After the heating process, the furnace was left to cool down to room temperature under N2 flow.

The synthesis of MoS2-F was done via hydrothermal synthesis according to the methodology reported by Nagaraju et al. [43]. First, an amount of AHMo was dissolved in deionized water and left under magnetic stirring for 10 min. Then TU and citric acid were added to the reaction mixture until complete dissolution and the resultant solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was transferred into an oven and maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. After naturally cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, afterward; it was dried at 90 °C. A thermal post-treatment at 180 °C during 30 min was applied to eliminate possible organic remnants without affecting the obtained flower morphology.

The morphologies of the as-obtained MoS2-R and MoS2-F and the modified electrodes with MoS2-R and MoS2-F were studied using a Nova NanoSEM 200 (FEI Company, USA) Scanning Electron Microscope with an acceleration voltage of 15 kV. X-ray diffraction patterns recorded from 5° to 70° were obtained by an Empyrean (PANalytical, USA) X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using monochromatic AlKα radiation (hν = 1486.7 eV), the deconvolution of the XPS spectra was done with the AAnalyzer 1.20 software, performing a Voigt peak fit and a Shirley-Sherwood background subtraction.

Firstly, different solutions containing 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 4 mg/mL of MoS2-R and MoS2-F were prepared and sonicated for 45 min. After a homogeneous solution was obtained, 10 µL of each solution were drop-casted onto the carbon paper (CP) electrode surface (0.5 cm x 0.5 cm) and were left to dry naturally at room temperature. For enzyme immobilization, the procedure reported by Moore et al. was followed as it has been proven that there is an increase in the electrochemical flux of redox couples through the membrane and the stability of the enzyme is improved resulting in an enhanced activity by using a mixture of Nafion:TBAB [44,45]. For the enzymes LacI and LacII, solutions of the purified enzyme (10 mg/mL) were mixed with a solution of Nafion/TBAB to obtain a solution ratio of 3:1 (enzyme:Nafion/TBAB) and the solution was vortexed at 2000 rpm for 1 min and then sonicated for 15 s. For the enzyme TvL, a solution containing 10 mg/mL of the enzyme was prepared and then it was mixed with a solution of Nafion/TBAB to obtain a solution ratio of 3:1 (enzyme:Nafion/TBAB), vortexed at 2000 rpm for 1 min and then sonicated for 15 s. After a homogeneous solution was obtained, 10 µL were taken and drop-casted onto the pristine CP electrodes and the CP electrodes modified with the MoS2 materials, to obtain the CP-Lac or CP-MoS2-Lac working electrodes, respectively.

Amperometric measurements were carried out on a CHI 611E Electrochemical Workstation (Shanghai, CH Instruments, China) with a conventional three-electrode system. Carbon paper electrode or modified CP electrode was used as the working electrode, saturated calomel as a reference electrode (SCE) and a platinum mesh as a counter electrode. All experiments were conducted at room temperature in a small Pyrex beaker containing 15 mL of a 0.1 M citric acid/KOH buffer (pH 4). The steady-state amperometric response of the CP-Lac and CP-MoS2-Lac at different DA concentrations was determined by successive additions of 1 M or 1 mM DA solution in deionized water. Firstly, the CP-Lac or CP-MoS2-Lac was equilibrated in 0.1 M citrate buffer at a constant potential of −0.2 V until a constant current was obtained, called background current (I1). Then, aliquots of the DA solution were added to the electrochemical cell and the obtained steady-state current response was recorded (I2). The obtained current difference (∆I = I2–I1) was used to plot a calibration curve of ∆I vs concentration of dopamine ([DA]). All measurements were done by triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity test for the laccase MoS2modified electrodes towards DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity test for modified electrodes was assessed in citric acid/KOH buffer (pH 4) at a potential of −0.2 V by using known dopamine interfering substances like ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glucose (Glu) [35]. These interfering substances were added in a concentration of 100 µM which is in accordance with those physiologically relevant concentrations found in urine [46–48]. The effect of interfering substances was evaluated by comparing the current modification to a 5 µM DA concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of dopamine in synthetic urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical effectiveness of the modified electrodes in a real environment, CP-MoS2-LacII and CP-MoS2-TvL electrodes were prepared according to the optimization of modified electrodes where the best results were achieved using MoS2 at a concentration of 2 mg/mL. Synthetic urine was composed of 0.2 g KCl, 5 g NaCl, 1.14 g Na2HPO4, 0.2 g NaH2PO4 and 21 g of urea, all dissolved in 1 L of ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm), then the pH was adjusted to 3.5 using H2SO4 (1 M). The amount of salts used were similar to those typically found in real urine samples [31]. For the electrochemical detection of DA successive injections of DA (10 mM) were made to a 15 mL synthetic urine solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared MoS2-R and MoS2-F
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM images of the as-obtained MoS2-R and MoS2-F are shown in Fig. 1 (a, b) and (c, d), respectively. From Fig. 1a it can be seen that the ribbons have an average length of 5 µm and an average width of 0.4 µm; moreover, in Fig. 1b it can be seen that the surface of the ribbons contains some nano-platelets that were grown over the surface. This is an interesting feature because such nano-platelets can serve as electroactive sites that are more accessible for the substrate to undergo a reduction reaction. On the other hand, as can be seen in Fig. 1c, the flowers don't have a well-defined morphology and present some degree of agglomeration forming agglomerates with diameters ranging from 2 to 5 µm. The surface of the flowers is composed of nano-walls that are folded and cross-linked together providing its surface with roughness (Fig. 1d), which could increase the electroactive surface area and thereby increase the number of sites for reduction of the substrate.

The XRD patterns of MoS2-R and MoS2-F are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, respectively. From Fig. 2a it can be seen that all diffraction peaks of the MoS2-R sample correspond to MoS2 structure (PDF card 00-006-0097) without any impurities. The characteristic peaks at 14.32°, 28.86°, 33.19°, 39.90°, 43.84°, 49.40°, 58.61° and 59.74° corresponding to the planes (002), (004), (100), (103), (006), (105), (008) and (112) are in agreement with a hexagonal crystal structure (2H) of MoS2, belonging to the space group P63/mmc (No. 194) [49]. The strong (002) peak (2θ = 14.32°) is indicative that the MoS2-R has a layer-stacked structure [50]. The Bragg's equation was applied to the (002) reflection in order to obtain the distance between MoS2 layers, denoted as d. In order to evaluate the average height stacked layers, denoted as H, the Scherrer's equation with a constant B = 0.89 [51] was applied to the (002) reflection. It was obtained that the interplanar distance was of d = 0.61 nm which is very close to its theoretical distance of 0.65 nm [20] and the obtained average height of the stacking layers was of H = 17 nm. By obtaining the ratio between H/d it was determined that the ribbons are composed (on average) of 28 stacked sheets of MoS2. See supplementary material (S1) for the complete information of the values used in the Bragg's and Scherrer's equations.

Fig. 2b shows the diffractogram obtained for MoS2-F. In this case, only the diffraction peaks located at 14°, 33° and 59.1° correspond to the 2H crystal structure of MoS2, the peak located at 12.54° corresponds to the phase Mo15S19 and the peaks located at 18.84° and 29.65° correspond to impurities of ammonium sulfate produced during the synthesis through the oxidation of TU [52]. Particularly, the asymmetric shape of the (100) plane at 2θ = 33° is characteristic of random layer lattice structures indicating that the layers are displaced randomly with respect to one another [53]. Moreover, the slight displacement of the (002) plane to 2θ = 14° is presumably due to the imperfect stacking of the layers [54].

To further investigate the chemical structure of the MoS2-R and MoS2-F, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed in order to identify the types of bonds that are present in the crystals. Fig. 3 a and b, show the corresponding XPS spectra of MoS2-R for the S2p and Mo3d species, respectively. The peaks in the S2p spectrum, Fig. 3a, with binding energies (B.E.) of 161.93 and 163.12 eV correspond to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively. The small peaks with B.E. of 166.62 and 171.54 eV correspond to the surface plasmon effect of the S2p3/2 and are in agreement with those values reported in the literature [55]. The peaks in the Mo3d spectrum (Fig. 3b) with B.E. of 229.1 and 232.24 eV correspond to the Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 and are attributed to the Mo ion with an oxidation state of +4 corresponding to MoS2 [7]. The peak with a B.E. of 226.26 eV can be ascribed to the S2s of the MoS2 [56] and the small peak at 235.28 eV corresponds to the Mo3d3/2 attributed to Mo ions with an oxidation state of +6 present in MoO3 [55]. As can be seen from the spectra, the intensity of this peak is very low indicating that only small concentrations of MoO3 are present on the ribbons. This can be confirmed by the XRD results, where no diffraction peaks corresponding to the MoO3 phase were found.

Fig. 3c and d show the corresponding XPS spectra of the S2p and Mo3d, respectively, for MoS2-F. The peaks in S2p spectrum (Fig. 3c) with B.E. of 161.68 and 162.90 eV correspond to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively, present in MoS2. In this case, there is another peak at 163.90 eV which corresponds to the B.E. of the S–O bond present in the species MoOxSy, which suggests that the reaction by the hydrothermal method was not complete. Moreover, the peaks with B.E. of 168.30 and 169.45 eV correspond to the bonds of SO species present in (NH4)2SO4 [57], these two peaks indicate that material has some impurities produced during the synthesis, this is in agreement with the results obtained from the XRD. The peaks in the Mo3d spectrum (Fig. 3d) with B.E. of 228.90 and 232 eV correspond to the Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 and are attributed to the Mo ion with an oxidation state of +4 [7]. The peak with a B.E. of 226.26 eV can be ascribed to the S2s of the MoS2 [56]. The peaks with B.E. of 232.93, 234.78 and 236.04 eV correspond to the bonds Mo–O present in MoOxSy. This is in agreement with the results obtained for the species present in S2p, which indicate that the MoS2-F has some impurities in the form of MoOxSy [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the modified working electrodes with MoS2-R and MoS2-F
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary studies revealed that electrodes modified only by nanomaterials without laccases did not give a significant analytical signal for the detection of dopamine (data not shown); meaning that biological components play an important role in the detection of the analyzed compound since the selectivity of the biosensor is conferred by enzyme, which is known to act on phenolic compounds [28]. In this study, laccase enzymes in equal concentration (10 mg/mL) were used.

In order to determine the optimal concentration of MoS2-R and MoS2-F for the best performance of the working electrodes, six different concentrations (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 4 mg/mL) were used to modify the electrodes. This was evaluated by obtaining the kinetic parameters of the LacI, LacII and TvL in the detection of dopamine in a range from 0.01 to 5 mM. Amperometric measurements were carried out in 0.1 M citric acid/KOH buffer (pH 4) at a constant potential of −0.2 V vs SCE; representative amperometric curves for 2 mg/mL of MoS2-R modified electrodes (Fig. 4 ) showed evident electrocatalytic effect obtained after successive additions of DA.

Fig. 5 shows the behavior of the LacI, LacII and TvL enzymes when tested with the electrodes modified with 1, 2 and 4 mg/mL of MoS2-R. The modification by using concentrations from 0.1 to 0.5 mg/mL of the nanomaterial did not cause an increase in the detection response of dopamine (data not shown). As can be seen, LacI (Fig. 5a), LacII (Fig. 5c) and TvL (Fig. 5e) present a Michaelis–Menten behavior when plotting current densities (J) vs. [DA] for the three concentrations of MoS2-R used. From there it can be observed an increase in the values of Jmax and a decrease in the Km values (Table 1 ) which indicates that the affinity of the enzymes for dopamine is increasing with the use of the nanomaterial. From those values, it seems that the three concentrations of MoS2-R have the same effect when improving the activity of the enzymes, but when analyzing the low concentration range, from 0.01 to 0.05 mM of dopamine (LacI Fig. 5b, LacII Fig. 5d and TvL Fig. 5f), it can be seen that in all cases as the concentration of MoS2-R is increasing there is an intensification in the response of the electrodes. From this information, it was concluded that the best concentration for the modification of the electrodes with MoS2-R was 2 mg/mL.

Fig. 6 shows the behavior of the enzymes LacI, LacII and TvL when tested with the electrodes modified with 0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 mg/mL of MoS2-F, concentrations from 1 to 4 mg/mL of the nanomaterial did not cause an increase in the detection response of dopamine (data not shown). As it can be seen for high concentrations of dopamine, the enzymes LacI (Fig. 6a), LacII (Fig. 6c) and TvL (Fig. 6e) present a Michaelis-Menten behavior, when plotting J vs. [DA], for all concentrations of MoS2-F used. In this case, the enzymes LacI and TvL present the best performance with the electrodes that were modified with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL of MoS2-F, as it can be seen from the increase in the values of Jmax and a decrease in the value of Km , Table 2 , and from the plots in the range of low concentrations from 0.01 to 0.05 mM of dopamine (LacI Fig. 6b and TvL Fig. 6f). On the other hand, the enzyme LacII presents the best performance with the electrodes that were modified with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL of MoS2-F, this can also be confirmed from the values of Jmax and Km from Table 2 and the plot in the range of low concentrations from 0.01 to 0.05 mM of dopamine (Fig. 6d).

These results suggest that the nature of the enzymes plays an important role in the mechanism of detection of dopamine as has been described previously [26]. Also, this kind of behavior where there is an increase in the enzymatic activity when coupled to different nanomaterials has been observed in other studies [59–61]. In the case of LacI and LacII, there is a considerable difference between the Jmax and Km when comparing the electrodes where only laccase enzyme was used for its modification and as the electrodes were modified with the MoS2 materials, their efficiency was improved reaching similar values as those of the TvL enzyme. This is an important feature since it makes possible the utilization of enzymes with low affinities for the substrate of interest but increased resistance to inhibitors (such as LacI and LacII) to be used in the analysis of samples that present inhibitory species such as ions or organic solvents.

When analyzing the best concentrations tested for the different morphologies of MoS2 it can be deduced that these morphologies also have a huge effect on the performance of the electrodes. One important feature is the contact at the interface between MoS2 and the electrode surface. When electrodes were modified with 2 mg/mL of MoS2-R (Fig. S2a), the material is well distributed throughout the surface and there are no visible gaps between the stacked MoS2 ribbons when analyzing it at high magnification (Fig. S2b). As depicted in Fig. S2c, due to the morphology of the ribbons, as the concentration is increased they tend to stack in an ordered manner assuring a good contact between adjacent ribbons and the surface of the electrode suggesting that the electron transfer process is favored.

On the other hand, when electrodes were modified with 0.5 mg/mL of MoS2-F (Fig. S2d), the material was also well distributed throughout the surface. Nonetheless, when analyzing it at high magnification, there are some visible gaps between the agglomerated MoS2 flowers (Fig. S2e) that should result in an inhibited electron transfer process. As illustrated in Fig. S2f, due to the shape of the flowers, as the concentration is increased, there are some gaps generated (indicated by red arrows) because of the agglomeration they present causing a decrease between the contact of the surface of the electrode and the flowers and instead of enhancing the electron transfer process, it is inhibited. Also, from the results obtained by XRD and XPS analysis, it is evident that due to the presence of secondary phases such as MoOxSy and (NH4)2SO4, the conductivity of MoS2-F may be hindered and the overall electrochemical performance of the electrodes is decreased. Supplementary Material S3 shows the CVs of CP, CP-MoS2-R (2 mg/mL) and CP-MoS2-F (0.5 mg/mL), where the possible remaining species by the incomplete reaction in the CP-MoS2-F synthesis showed electroactive characteristic presenting a cathodic peak around −0.38 V and anodic peak at 0.2 V that may interfere with the redox process of the developed laccase biosensor and thus reducing its detection performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of dopamine with optimally modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to characterize the MoS2-R (2 mg/mL) modified electrodes for dopamine detection, cyclic voltammetry in 0.1 M citric acid/KOH buffer (pH 4) at a scan rate of 50 mV/s was performed for LacI, LacII and TvL in the absence and in presence of 200 µM of dopamine. As shown in Fig. 7 , results for electrodes in absence of dopamine no redox response was observed at the potential range from −0.2 to +0.6 V. After dopamine was added, a pair of small redox peaks appeared at the CVs of all electrodes with anodic peak and cathodic peak at 0.45 V and 0.15 V, respectively. The appearance of these peaks in CV curves demonstrate laccases had been immobilized on the modified electrode surface, thus retaining the laccase biocatalytic activity [62,63]. The electron transfer mechanism for CP-MoS2-Lac electrode occurs as follows: dopamine is oxidized, in the presence of oxygen, by the enzyme to its respective dopamine-o-quinone (DOQ) form, then DOQ gets electrochemically reduced at the surface of the electrode back to its DA transferring electrons to the electrode surfaces which gets reflected as an electrical current, Fig. S4, [64,65].

Based on the optimum concentrations tested of MoS2-R and MoS2-F used for the modification of the electrodes, further analysis was performed to determine the linear ranges and limits of detection for dopamine. It was found that the electrodes modified with 2 mg/mL of MoS2-R (in the case of the three enzymes) exhibited two linear ranges for dopamine, the first from 0.1 to 0.5 µM and the second from 1 to 5 µM. Electrodes modified with 0.1 mg/mL of MoS2-F (for LacI and TvL) and 0.5 mg/mL of MoS2-F (for LacII) did not enhance the response at such low concentrations (data not shown). The calibration curves obtained for the linear ranges using electrodes modified with 2 mg/mL of MoS2-R, LacI, LacII and TvL as well as the linear ranges obtained for the electrodes that were only modified with the enzymes are shown in Fig. 8 .

From this data, it can be seen that in all cases the sensitivity of the electrodes was greatly increased when upon modification with MoS2-R and the enzymes that show the highest increase are LacII (5 fold) and LacI (3.2 fold) when detecting dopamine in the range of concentrations from 0.1 to 0.5 µM. Table 3 shows the corresponding sensitivities and correlation coefficients for the different calibration curves obtained.

Limits of detection (LOD) were determined by increasing the concentration of dopamine gradually until a statistically significant response in current was observed. The obtained values for the electrodes modified with the three enzymes (CP-Lac) and electrodes modified with each of the three enzymes and MoS2-R (CP-MoS2-Lac) was 10 nM, which to the best of our knowledge is the lowest reported when compared with other laccase-based electrochemical biosensors for the detection of dopamine, as compared in Table 4 .

From these results, it can be concluded that the MoS2-R has a huge impact on the improvement of the sensitivity of the biosensor. In all cases the sensitivity of the biosensor when analyzing low concentrations of dopamine [0.1 to 0.5 µM] increased by 3.2, 5 and 2.45 fold for LacI, LacII and TvL, respectively, when compared to the biosensors that did not have the nanomaterial present (Fig. 5). When comparing the results obtained in this work with others reported in the literature (Table 4), it is evident that when the enzymes LacI and LacII are coupled to MoS2-R, the biosensors present the lowest LOD and higher sensitivities reported in literature.

Determination of the enzymatic kinetics for free and immobilized laccases are found in Supplementary Material S5. Laccases in their free form showed KMapp (µM) of 42.07 ± 3.136, 16.64 ± 2.548 and 34.51 ± 5.857 for TvL, LacI and LacII respectively. It was found that after immobilization onto MoS2 modified carbon paper by using TBAB-Nafion, a decrease in substrate affinity (ABTS) of laccase from TvL (123 ± 26.96 µM) and Lac I (58.68 ± 10.12 µM) was observed, increasing its KMapp by 1.93 and 2.41 fold respectively. Conversely, this effect was not observed when LacII (35.57 ± 4.79 µM) was immobilized, as no-significant changes in its KMapp value were detected. Affinity reduction for TvL and LacI may be a consequence of the diffusional limitations of the substrate by the enzyme, or conformational changes of the enzyme created by the entrapment onto polymer micelles, hindering the interaction among substrate and laccase [69,70]. These results are in accordance with the observed sensitivity behavior, where laccases TvL and LacI exhibited lower sensitivities than LacII.

Moreover, the possible release of immobilized laccase enzymes on the electrodes was evaluated (Supplementary Material S6), and the results showed that in the first cycle, the released percentage of enzyme activity (%) was 5.58 ± 0.29, 7.75 ± 1.11 and 6.48 ± 1.25 for electrodes modified with TvL, LacI and LacII respectively. Released enzyme activity for the second cycle and onwards was around 0.2% to non-detectable. These results can be attributed to the fact that some enzymes remained free after the TBAB-Nafion immobilization process; this behavior is similar to previously published works where unbound enzymes were released [45,69,70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of bioelectrodes towards DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: As observed in Fig. 9 , the signal obtained after adding DA (5 µM) was not affected by the injection of Glu and UA, and this behavior was observed for the three enzymes. Similar results have been reported in literature, and have been attributed to the fact that the applied potential for laccase-modified electrodes is below the redox potential for UA [71,72], and the fact that glucose is not a laccase substrate [73]. In contrast, when AA was added to the reaction, signal changes were observed and differed for each enzyme. Specifically, for TvL the signal increased, while for LacI the signal decreased. Since laccases and ascorbate oxidases are enzymes structurally and mechanistically similar, it is possible that AA acts as a substrate for both. It has been also suggested by others authors that AA act as a competitive inhibitor [74,75].

In the case of LacII, the addition of AA caused negligible changes. LacII proved to have better selectivity compared to TvL, a commercially available enzyme from a different source (T. versicolor), and remarkably to its isoform (LacI), which was obtained from the same organism (P. sanguineus). The results of these experiments highlight the relevance of carrying out research regarding the assessment of new enzymes that can improve biosensing systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of dopamine in synthetic urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Table 5 summarizes the behavior of the electrodes modified with LacII and TvL and 2 mg/mL of MoS2-R. Here, it can be seen that electrodes modified with LacII present a lower limit of detection, as it would be expected as one of the interesting characteristics about this enzyme is that it presents an enhanced resistance to common inhibitors such as Cl− ions and urea, which are normally found in urine. The linearity results are also presented in Table 5, where it can be observed that TvL has a slightly better sensibility than LacII. Despite the LOD for LacII is lower by 2 µM confirming that this enzyme is a better candidate to be used in practical applications as the detection of DA in real urine samples. This can also be confirmed by the R2 values, where LacII presented better linearity. Another interesting feature is that the standard deviation is lower for the measurements conducted with enzyme LacII which can be related to a more accurate measurement. Calibration curves and representative amperometric curves for the determination of DA in synthetic urine can be found in Supplementary Material S6. According to these results, the use of a novel laccase based electrochemical biosensor can be a powerful tool for the determination of any noticeable increase in normal DA concentrations in urine samples (0.3–2.6 μM/day), which could be indicative of diseases like the presence of pheochromocytoma, paraganglioma and neuroblastoma tumors [34–37]. Also, DA determination via a laccase based biosensor has the advantage of being a fast non-invasive method with good performance that can be more convenient for the detection of this biomarker [36].

A novel amperometric biosensor (CP-MoS2-R-Nafion:TBAB-Lac) was successfully developed by the modification of the electrode surface with molybdenum disulfide in the form of ribbons (MoS2-R) and flowers (MoS2-F), coupled with the immobilization of laccase enzymes. The resulting biosensor exhibited good analytical performance in the determination of dopamine in citric acid/KOH buffer, providing high sensitivity (340.3 nA/µM) and a low LOD (10 nM). It was demonstrated that the morphology and the nature of the MoS2 nanomaterials have a great effect on the performance of the system and that they have a positive effect when coupled with the laccase enzymes. It was also observed that the MoS2-R morphology causes an enhancement in electrode sensibility allowing the detection of dopamine in very low concentrations (10 nM). The two isoforms of the laccase enzyme from the white-rot fungi Pycnoporus sanguineus CS43 (LacI and LacII) were successfully employed for the first time in the construction of a novel electrochemical biosensor giving the characteristic of selectivity to phenolic-like compounds.

It was also confirmed that the enzyme LacII has an enhanced resistance to Cl− ions and urea inhibitors as our research group stated before. This enhanced resistances allowed LacII to have a LOD of 0.67 µM in a synthetic urine sample with a high concentration of Cl–, it also presented a good linear range of detection ranging from 1.33 to 13.32 µM. Furthermore, LacII showed negligible signal modification to interfering substances such as glucose, uric acid and ascorbic acid. These features make LacII biosensor a potential candidate to be used as a powerful and fast tool for the determination in real urine samples of any noticeable increase in normal dopamine concentrations. Nevertheless, further research is required in order to apply the developed biosensor to real samples.
